3A_Bibliographic.citation: Song, K., Zhang, J.-L., Zhou, N., Fu, Y., Zou, B., Xu, L.-X., Wang, Z., Li, X., Zhao, Y., Potenza, M., Fang, X., & Zhang, J.-T. (2023). Youth screen media activity patterns and associations with behavioral developmental measures and resting-state brain functional connectivity. Journal of the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry, 62(9), 1051-1063.
3A_Bibliographic.journal: Journal of the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry

3C_Exposure.exposure_measure: Youth Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ) - 14 items; K-means clustering to identify SMA patterns
3C_Exposure.how_operationalized: K-means clustering on 14 STQ items identified 2 subgroups: higher-frequency SMA pattern (subgroup 1, n=3,151, 26.7%) characterized by more TV/videos/games, and lower-frequency SMA pattern (subgroup 2, n=8,664, 73.3%). Binary SMA subgroup membership used as predictor in LME models.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Behavioral developmental measures (neurocognition, mental health, traitlike measures) and resting-state brain functional connectivity (RSFC)
3D_Outcome.measure_used: NIH Toolbox (21 neurocognitive measures); CBCL (mental health); UPPS-P Impulsive Behavior Scale and BIS/BAS (traitlike); resting-state fMRI functional connectivity (338 RSFCs across 13 cortical networks and 19 subcortical regions)

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Cross-sectional: SMA subgroup (dummy-coded) as fixed-effect predictor; site and family nested within site as random intercepts. Population weights applied. FDR correction. Longitudinal: 3-level conditional growth model with time as wave, random slopes and intercepts at site and nested individual level. 21 + 22 + 338 models for cross-sectional; time x subgroup interaction for longitudinal.
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: Age, sex/gender, race/ethnicity, handedness, parental marital status, parental highest education, family income, household size, household structure; for RSFC: also handedness and mean framewise displacement
3E_Design_Analysis.SES_in_model: yes - family income and parental highest education included
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Given the nested structure of the ABCD data, 3-level LME models were used to test differences in developmental measures (21 regression models for neurocognitive and traitlike measures, 22 for mental health-related measures, 338 for RSFCs) between identified SMA subgroups.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Higher-frequency SMA subgroup vs lower: cognition total composite score (adjusted)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Subgroup 1 showed poorer performance on multiple neurocognitive assessments and the cognition total composite score (standardized beta = -.12, 95% CI [-0.08, -0.16], FDR-corrected p < .001)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Higher-frequency SMA subgroup: total behavioral problems (CBCL TotProb)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: higher scores on total behavioral problems (CBCL TotProb: standardized beta = .13, 95% CI [0.09, 0.17], FDR-corrected p < .001)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Higher-frequency SMA subgroup: prodromal psychotic symptoms severity score (PPS)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: and more psychotic-like experiences (prodromal psychotic symptoms severity score: standardized beta = .31, 95% CI [0.26, 0.35], FDR-corrected p < .001)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Higher-frequency SMA subgroup: UPPS-P impulsivity sum score
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: subgroup 1 was associated with higher impulsivity (UPPS-P sum score: standardized beta = .36, 95% CI [0.32, 0.41], FDR-corrected p < .001)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Higher-frequency SMA subgroup: BIS sum score
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: BIS sum score (standardized beta = .16, 95% CI [0.11, 0.20], FDR-corrected p < .001)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Higher-frequency SMA subgroup: BAS sum score
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: BAS sum score (standardized beta = .34, 95% CI [0.29, 0.38], FDR-corrected p < .001)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: RSFC between salience network and left ventral DC (largest RSFC effect)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: The RSFC between the salience network and left ventral DC (ventral diencephalon) had the largest effect size (standardized beta = .13, 95% CI [0.08, 0.18], FDR-corrected p < .001).

3G_Interpretation.verbatim_recommendation: The findings suggest that public health strategies to decrease excessive time spent by children on video entertainment-related SMA are needed. Further studies are needed to examine potential video-centric/social communication-centric SMA bifurcation to understand dynamic changes and trajectories of SMA patterns and related outcomes developmentally. Such information may help inform the updating of screen use guidelines and related interventions for youth in the contemporary digital technology environment.

3H_Rhetorical.pattern_detail: The paper appropriately reports moderate-to-small standardized betas with CIs and FDR correction. Some effects are genuinely moderate (impulsivity beta=0.36, BAS=0.34, psychotic-like experiences=0.31). However, some RSFC and cognitive effects are small (beta=0.12-0.13). The paper notes that 'statistical significance was sensitive to covariate adjustment' for RSFC and that 'some RSFCs with small effect sizes were not significant in the adjusted model.' They then recommend public health strategies to decrease screen time. The combination of measured interpretation and policy recommendations reflects a hybrid pattern.

3I_Flags.ambiguity_notes: This paper uses K-means clustering to identify SMA patterns rather than continuous screen time. The 'higher-frequency' subgroup is characterized primarily by more video entertainment, not social media per se. Cross-sectional design at baseline for the main findings (behavioral and RSFC). Longitudinal analyses show persistence of some but not all effects over 2 years. HCP-D replication was only partial. Many of the 338+ tests have very small effects despite surviving FDR. The paper appropriately notes limitations including self-report and the preliminary nature of longitudinal analyses.
